title: Data Update 4 for 2026: The Global Perspective!
gist: Damodaran's global data update argues US equity dominance is slipping (47% of world market cap but only 38% of 2025's gain), reframes country risk through a corporate-life-cycle lens, shows sovereign ratings lag reality versus CDS markets, and lays out his post-US-downgrade equity risk premium and currency-based riskfree rate methodology.

insights.claim: US equities returned 17.72% in 2025 while the US dollar weakened by more than 7% during the year, so the currency move materially changed how attractive US returns looked to non-dollar investors.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: A weak dollar can erase much of a strong US equity return for foreign holders, arguing for hedging or currency-aware allocation decisions.
insights.locator: Global Equties in 2025

insights.claim: India was the worst-performing sub-region in 2025 with dollar returns of only 3.31%, driven by both a below-average local return (Sensex up 8.55%) and rupee depreciation, confirming Damodaran's earlier view that the India growth story would face a rockier path than its bulls expected.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Investors betting on the India growth narrative should expect currency drag and volatility, not a smooth compounding story.
insights.locator: As I mentioned in my second data update, India was the worst performing

insights.claim: US equities held 47% of global market capitalization at the start of 2026, but the US captured only 38% of the total increase in global market cap during 2025, with Europe and China taking disproportionately large shares of the gain — early evidence that US market dominance is starting to slip.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: A widening gap between US market-cap share and US share of incremental gains is an early signal to watch for a slow rotation of capital toward Europe and China.
insights.locator: Zeroing in on the aggregate market capitalization

insights.claim: The premature 'epitaph' many US investors and wealth advisors were writing for international diversification a year ago was wrong: two decades of US outperformance is not proof that global diversification is permanently obsolete, and even though S&P 500 firms get almost 60% of revenue abroad, 2025's US underperformance versus the rest of the world shows genuine international diversification still belongs in a prudent investor's toolkit.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Investors who abandoned non-US allocations on the 'US always wins' thesis should reconsider, since 2025 already broke the pattern.
insights.quote: an epitaph for international diversification that many US investors, wealth advisors and market experts were starting to etch in stone even a year ago
insights.locator: To close this section, I will unwrite

themes.theme: Country risk as a life-cycle problem

insights.claim: Democracies create continuous, low-grade policy uncertainty because government turnover brings recurring policy change, while autocracies create near-term policy stability but carry a greater risk of catastrophic, wrenching uncertainty later on.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Investors should not treat 'stable autocracy' as inherently lower risk than 'noisy democracy' — the risk shows up on a different, longer time horizon.
insights.locator: Political Structure

insights.claim: Corruption functions as an implicit tax on businesses exposed to it and also undercuts trust and willingness to follow rules more broadly, and a meaningful share of cross-country corruption differences comes from structures deliberately designed to encourage and reward it, not just culture.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Screening country risk on corruption should look at institutional/structural incentives, not just cultural reputation, since the former is what can change with policy.
insights.locator: Corruption

insights.claim: Damodaran applies his corporate life-cycle framework to countries: young economies have high growth potential but more volatility and need strong governance to deliver on it; middle-aged economies get more stability but slower growth, with governance mattering less; and aging economies can stagnate or shrink outright as they lose competitive edge and their populations age.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Gives investors a structural lens for classifying countries beyond emerging/developed labels — matching governance and growth expectations to a country's economic 'age.'
insights.locator: Young economies have higher growth potential

insights.claim: Damodaran suggests Trump's 2025 policy actions can be read, at least partly, as nostalgia for a past era when the US uniquely combined strong economic growth with stability, a pairing few other economies in history have matched — an application of his country-life-cycle lens to current US politics.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Frames US policy unpredictability (tariffs, etc.) as a structural symptom of a maturing economy trying to reclaim youthful growth, useful context for anticipating further nationalist/protectionist moves.
insights.locator: it may be worth thinking about the Trump actions in 2025

themes.theme: Sovereign ratings lag reality; CDS moves faster

insights.claim: Sovereign credit ratings focus almost entirely on default risk and can diverge sharply from broader political risk — Damodaran singles out Saudi Arabia as a country with low or no default risk yet large exposure to political risk.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Investors should not use a country's credit rating as a proxy for overall political/business risk in the Middle East and similar cases — the two can decouple.
insights.quote: have low or no default risk, but remain exposed to large political risks
insights.locator: The sovereign ratings are focused almost entirely

insights.claim: Sovereign ratings are structurally slow to react to change: Argentina and Venezuela saw significant shifts in governance and politics over the past year, yet their agency ratings have not changed or barely changed.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Ratings-based country risk measures can be stale during fast-moving political change, so investors should cross-check with faster market-based signals like CDS.
insights.locator: The sovereign ratings have their share of biases

insights.claim: The 2025 US sovereign ratings downgrade was the most attention-grabbing ratings change of the year; markets largely absorbed it calmly, but it forced Damodaran to alter his own equity risk premium estimation process.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Even a 'calmly absorbed' US downgrade has downstream effects on standard valuation inputs (equity risk premiums) used across the industry.
insights.locator: There were a few ratings changes in 2025

insights.claim: For the roughly 80 countries with a sovereign CDS market, default spreads are market-priced and update faster than agency ratings; both Argentina and Venezuela CDS-implied spreads improved in 2025, moving from effectively uninsurable to merely very costly to insure.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: CDS spreads are a more timely alternative to ratings for tracking fast-changing country risk in real time, useful for EM debt/equity positioning.
insights.locator: For the 80 countries where sovereign CDS exist

themes.theme: Equity risk premium methodology after the US downgrade

insights.claim: Because of the US sovereign ratings downgrade, Damodaran no longer uses the S&P 500's implied equity risk premium directly as the 'mature market' premium; he now strips out the US's own default spread (0.23%) from it, arriving at a mature market equity risk premium of 4.23% as of early 2026.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Anyone using Damodaran-style ERP inputs in DCF models should adopt the revised 4.23% mature-market baseline rather than the old raw S&P 500 implied premium.
insights.locator: It is undeniable that the ratings downgrade for the US

insights.claim: Country-level equity risk premiums are built by scaling each country's ratings-based default spread up for equities' relative riskiness and adding it to the 4.23% mature-market premium, with frontier (unrated) countries folded in via country risk scores instead of ratings.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Gives a replicable, updated method for estimating discount rates for any country, including unrated frontier markets, as of start-2026 data.
insights.locator: To get to country risk premiums for individual countries

insights.claim: Government bond rates should not be used directly as riskfree rates, contrary to what standard finance textbooks teach, because governments do sometimes default on local-currency bonds rather than devalue; the default-risk component must be stripped out of the government bond rate first.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Practitioners using raw local government bond yields as riskfree rates in valuation are overstating true riskfree rates by embedding default risk.
insights.locator: In many finance classes and textbooks

insights.claim: After removing default risk, riskfree rates vary widely across currencies primarily due to differences in expected inflation: very low for Swiss Franc, Japanese yen and Thai Baht; moderate for US dollar and Euro; and very high for Turkish Lira and Zambian Kwacha.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Currency selection for financing/discounting should account for embedded inflation expectations, not just nominal rate levels.
insights.locator: After this clean-up, you can see that riskfree rates vary widely

insights.claim: Damodaran predicts it is unlikely US treasury bond rates will drop much below 4% as long as inflation keeps running hot in the 2.5-3% range, and he extends this same inflation-differential logic to build riskfree rate estimates in any currency, even those without a local-currency government bond.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: A floor near 4% on US treasuries under sustained inflation is a concrete rate-path view relevant to duration positioning and discount-rate assumptions.
insights.quote: unlikely that you will see US treasury bond rates drop much below 4%, at least as long as inflation runs hot (2.5-3%)
insights.locator: The logic that I used to argue

insights.claim: Worked example: the Egyptian pound riskfree rate at the start of 2026 is derived as the US dollar riskfree rate (3.95%, i.e. the 4.18% US 10-year Treasury rate minus the 0.23% US default spread) plus the inflation differential between Egypt (7.78%) and the US (2.24%), yielding approximately 9.49% (9.58% on a compounded basis).
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Provides a replicable template for deriving a local-currency riskfree rate anywhere using only a US Treasury rate and two inflation forecasts.
insights.locator: To illustrate, I will try to estimate an Egyptian pound

themes.theme: Currency invariance and the real effect of inflation on value

insights.claim: Damodaran's 'currency invariance theorem' holds that the value of a project or company should not change merely because the analysis is redone in a different currency, as long as inflation assumptions are applied consistently to both cash flows and discount rates — a positive-NPV project in dollars stays positive-NPV in Egyptian pounds, and an overvalued company stays overvalued.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Analysts switching valuation currency to make a company look cheaper or a project look better are fooling themselves — the underlying value doesn't move if assumptions are internally consistent.
insights.quote: the value of a project or company should not change, if you change the currency in which you do your analysis
insights.locator: Put simply, the effects of expected inflation

insights.claim: Being consistently wrong about expected inflation does not distort a valuation, but actual changes in inflation can still change a business's real value, with the impact depending on whether the company has pricing power to pass rising costs through to customers.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Investors should screen for pricing power as the key differentiator of which companies survive high-inflation regimes with value intact.
insights.locator: that does not mean that changes in inflation cannot change the values

insights.claim: High Turkish lira inflation has genuinely destroyed value at some Turkish companies, and simply revaluing those companies in Euros will not undo that real economic damage — the currency invariance theorem doesn't mean currency choice can launder away inflation-driven losses.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: A concrete warning against using currency conversion tricks to mask real inflation-driven value destruction in emerging-market holdings.
insights.quote: high inflation in Turkish lira has undoubtedly wreaked havoc the value of some Turkish companies
insights.locator: Thus, high inflation in Turkish lira

themes.theme: Globalization backlash as the master narrative

insights.claim: Damodaran frames the last four decades of global politics and economics as first driven by globalization as an almost unstoppable force shaping immigration and economic policy, and now by a backlash against it that has upended political power structures in both Europe and the United States and brought nationalist parties to power across many emerging market countries — a shift he believes is not reversible.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Investors should treat nationalist/protectionist policy (tariffs, immigration restrictions, delinking rhetoric) as a durable multi-year regime rather than a transient shock to fade.
insights.quote: I don't think that this genie is going back into the bottle
insights.locator: I am not a political or a macroeconomic analyst
